title: Serve indexed repo structure directly from the graph instead of requiring agents to grep
context: Agents exploring unfamiliar code waste multiple grep/glob/read rounds rediscovering structure and dependencies that the dependency graph already holds. This is inefficient—the structure is already indexed. Touches invariant(s): con_902759b3dc (All JSON writes to .hunch/ must be atomic (temp-file + rename) so an interrupted write can never truncate the index); con_a87360128b (Semantic vectors are a derived layer in SQLite, never the source of truth; reconcile by content hash on reindex); con_e04226bd05 (The MCP server must stay client-agnostic: all assistants point to the same .hunch/ graph; no Claude-only behavior in the server).
decision: Introduced `hunch_structure` tool that serves deterministic, multi-resolution answers directly from the graph: repo map (no target), directory contents (target dir), file outline (target file), or symbol definition with one-hop neighbors (exact name). Single interface, zero grep rounds, tells agents exactly which file to read first try.
consequences: Agents orient faster with fewer round-trips and deterministic results
Exploration becomes graph-first rather than text-search-first
Reduces cognitive load: agent asks 'what is this structure?' instead of running discover sequences
Couples navigation quality to index freshness—stale index gives wrong answers
alternatives_rejected: Keep grep as the exploration primitive (acknowledged as inefficient, hence 'anti-grep' framing)
Add fuzzy symbol search instead (hunch_query handles that separately; this is deterministic structure, not search)
Serve raw graph edges without aggregation (formatStructure handles agent-usability)

rejected_tripwires.alternative: Keep grep as the exploration primitive (acknowledged as inefficient, hence 'anti-grep' framing)
Add fuzzy symbol search instead (hunch_query handles that separately; this is deterministic structure, not search)
Serve raw graph edges without aggregation (formatStructure handles agent-usability)
